realistic_solution: Instead of forming the Hessian, apply the Linear time Stochastic Second-Order Algorithm (LiSSA) or Conjugate Gradient to compute an implicit Inverse Hessian-Vector Product (IHVP), reducing both time and memory complexity to $O(p)$.
napkin_math: Full Hessian memory: $(50 \times 10^6)^2 \times 4 \text{ bytes} = 10 \text{ PB}$. Exact inverse compute: $(50 \times 10^6)^3 = 1.25 \times 10^{23} \text{ FLOPs}$. On 100 A100s ($3.12 \times 10^{16} \text{ FLOPS}$ total), time = $1.25 \times 10^{23} / 3.12 \times 10^{16} \approx 4 \times 10^6 \text{ seconds}$ (>46 days). IHVP approximation reduces compute to $O(p)$, taking minutes instead of weeks on a single GPU.

title: Continuous vs Dynamic Batching Trade-offs
scenario: You are evaluating the architecture of an LLM inference server for an 8B parameter model deployed on NVIDIA A100 (80GB) GPUs. The service has a strict Service Level Objective (SLO): Time-to-First-Token (TTFT) < 200ms and Time-Between-Tokens (TBT) < 50ms at a concurrency of 100 requests/sec. The current implementation uses traditional dynamic batching (request-level scheduling) but experiences severe TTFT degradation (up to 3 seconds) during peak traffic due to highly variable input and output sequence lengths. Evaluate the architectural trade-offs between Request-Level Scheduling (Dynamic Batc
common_mistake: Recommending a drastic reduction in the maximum batch size to minimize latency, which destroys hardware utilization and leads to cascading queue buildup at high concurrency, or suggesting sequence padding which wastes memory and compute without solving the fundamental head-of-line blocking issue.
realistic_solution: Implement Iteration-Level Scheduling (Continuous Batching). This architecture allows the inference server to evict finished requests and schedule new requests at each token generation step (iteration), rather than waiting for all requests in a batch to complete. This resolves head-of-line blocking, decoupling the TTFT of new requests from the maximum generation length of currently executing requests.
napkin_math: Under dynamic batching, if a new request arrives right after a batch starts and the longest sequence in that batch is 100 tokens, it waits: 100 tokens * 40ms/token = 4,000ms (violating the 200ms TTFT SLO). With continuous batching, the scheduler admits the new request at the next token boundary, reducing the queue wait time to just 1 token step (40ms) + prefill execution time.

title: Redesigning an End-to-End Vision Inference Pipeline
scenario: A cloud-based real-time video analytics service processes 1080p streams using an object detection model on NVIDIA T4 GPUs. The SLA requires end-to-end latency under 100ms per frame. Currently, the pipeline is CPU-bottlenecked: preprocessing (resizing, normalization) takes 45ms on CPU, GPU inference takes 20ms, and post-processing (NMS, bounding box scaling) takes 40ms on CPU. Evaluate architectural approaches to redesign this pipeline to meet the SLA while maximizing overall throughput and GPU utilization. What are the quantitative trade-offs of your design?
common_mistake: Focusing solely on optimizing the neural network weights (e.g., using quantization or TensorRT) while ignoring the dominant CPU-bound pre- and post-processing bottlenecks, leaving the GPU severely underutilized.
realistic_solution: Offload preprocessing and post-processing to the GPU to eliminate CPU bottlenecks and reduce expensive PCIe data transfers. Utilize libraries like NVIDIA DALI for GPU-accelerated decoding/resizing and implement NMS as a GPU kernel or TensorRT plugin.
napkin_math: Baseline latency: Pre (45ms) + Inf (20ms) + Post (40ms) = 105ms (>100ms SLA). GPU Utilization = 20 / 105 = 19%. Optimized latency: GPU Pre (5ms) + Inf (20ms) + GPU Post (5ms) = 30ms. With a 30ms pipeline, we can process a batch size of 3 within the 100ms SLA (90ms total), increasing throughput by 3x and pushing GPU utilization towards 100%.

title: Evaluating Influence Functions for ViT Attribution
scenario: You are debugging false positives in a production ViT-L/16 (300M parameters) medical imaging model. The clinical team needs to identify which specific training images from the 5M image dataset are most responsible for these errors. Leave-one-out retraining takes 100 A100 GPU-hours per image. You are evaluating Influence Functions to estimate training data attribution. How do you assess the feasibility of computing the inverse Hessian-vector product (iHVP) for a 300M parameter model, and what design approximations are necessary to make it tractable in a production environment?
common_mistake: Candidates often propose computing the exact Hessian or using standard LiSSA across all weights, failing to realize that storing the full Hessian for a 300M parameter model requires petabytes of memory.
realistic_solution: Justify restricting the influence function computation to only the final dense classification head, or evaluate using Kronecker-factored (K-FAC) approximations for the full model. Applying IF to only the final 1024-dimensional layer reduces the Hessian to a trivial 4MB size while still providing highly correlated attribution signals.
napkin_math: Full Hessian memory = (300M * 300M) elements * 4 bytes/element = 3.6 * 10^17 bytes = ~360 PB. Last layer Hessian = (1024 * 1024) elements * 4 bytes = ~4.2 MB. Inverting a 1024x1024 matrix takes <10ms on a standard CPU.

title: Diagnosing Dynamic Batching Latency Spikes
scenario: You deploy a ResNet-152 classification model on an NVIDIA A10G using Triton Inference Server with dynamic batching. To maximize throughput, the team sets `max_batch_size=128` and `max_queue_delay=50ms`. During micro-bursts of 400 QPS, the server sustains the throughput and GPU utilization remains around 55%, but P99 latency degrades to 450ms, severely violating the 200ms SLA. Profiling shows memory is well within limits. Analyze the bottleneck.
common_mistake: Assuming the GPU lacks compute capacity or that network I/O is the bottleneck. Candidates often suggest increasing GPU count or lowering the queue delay, failing to realize that massive batch execution times cause severe head-of-line blocking.
realistic_solution: Profile the model's execution time across different batch sizes and strictly cap `max_batch_size` (e.g., to 32). The sum of `max_queue_delay` and the execution time of `max_batch_size` must be kept well below the target SLA to account for queue buildup during bursts.
napkin_math: If batch_size=128 takes 180ms to compute: A micro-burst of 200 requests creates a batch of 128. The remaining 72 requests must wait 180ms for the GPU to free up, plus their own compute time. Latency for the second batch = 180ms (wait) + 110ms (compute) = 290ms (SLA violated). By capping max_batch_size to 32 (compute time ~50ms), the first 32 finish in 50ms, the next in 100ms, and the 6th batch finishes around 300ms, drastically reducing the average and tail wait times.

title: Diagnosing Influence Function OOM
scenario: Your team is deploying a toxicity classifier based on RoBERTa-base (125M parameters) on an AWS p4d.24xlarge instance (8x A100 40GB GPUs). To debug a high-profile false positive, an engineer implements classical influence functions to find the training data most responsible. The job instantly crashes with a CUDA Out of Memory (OOM) error before completing even one training data evaluation step. You need to analyze the memory profile of this implementation and identify the root cause of the crash.
common_mistake: Assuming the OOM is caused by large batch sizes or activation memory, and attempting to fix it via gradient checkpointing or micro-batching without addressing the quadratic memory complexity of the Hessian.
realistic_solution: Identify that computing the full Hessian matrix for 125M parameters fundamentally exceeds hardware capacity. Resolve the issue by restricting the influence function computation to the final projection layer (e.g., computing the Hessian only for the 768x2 classification head) or by using implicit Inverse Hessian-Vector Product (IHVP) approximations like LiSSA.
napkin_math: Hessian matrix elements = P^2 = (125 * 10^6)^2 = 1.56 * 10^16. At FP32 (4 bytes per element), memory required = 1.56 * 10^16 * 4 = 62.5 * 10^15 bytes = 62.5 Petabytes. 62.5 PB is exponentially larger than the node's 320GB total GPU memory, making full Hessian storage impossible.

title: Diagnosing Input Stationary Bottlenecks in MLP Workloads
scenario: Your team is porting a heavy DLRM recommendation model (MLP layers of 4096x4096s) to a legacy cloud inference accelerator designed for CNNs. The chip uses a strict Input Stationary (IS) dataflow in its 64x64 MAC systolic array, with 2MB of local SRAM. At a batch size of 256, profiling shows the 800 GB/s DRAM bandwidth is fully saturated, but MAC utilization is a dismal 4%. Why is this specific dataflow causing the bottleneck, and how do you quantify the architectural mismatch?
common_mistake: Candidates often assume the problem is simply a 'memory-bound' matrix-vector multiplication without realizing that at Batch=256, the layer *should* be compute-bound. They fail to analyze how the IS dataflow forces catastrophic redundant loading of the weights.
realistic_solution: The Input Stationary architecture pins input activations in the MACs and streams weights. Since the 64x64 array only holds a tile of 64 inputs at once, a batch of 256 requires 4 passes. This means the massive 32MB weight matrix is redundantly loaded from DRAM 4 times per layer, thrashing bandwidth. The fix is to transpose the matrix formulation in software (switching inputs and weights) to trick the compiler into treating the massive weights as the 'stationary inputs'.
napkin_math: Weights = 4096 * 4096 * 2B (FP16) = 32MB. Array holds 64 input tokens. Input tiles = B256 / 64 = 4. Weight data loaded from DRAM = 4 tiles * 32MB = 128MB per layer. Total compute = 2 * 256 * 4096 * 4096 = 8.58 GFLOPs. Arithmetic Intensity = 8.58 GFLOPs / 128 MB = 67 FLOPs/Byte. At 800 GB/s bandwidth, max throughput = 800 * 67 = 53.6 TFLOPs/s, bottlenecking the chip to low single-digit utilization.

title: Input Stationary Dataflow Memory Math
scenario: You are optimizing a custom datacenter accelerator executing a linear projection layer Y = XW using INT8 precision. Input activations X have a shape of 128 tokens x 128 hidden_dim. Weights W have a shape of 128 hidden_dim x 4096 out_features. The accelerator features a 128x128 Processing Element (PE) array, with each PE containing exactly 1 byte of local register space (16 KB total capacity across the array). Assuming a simplified architecture with no intermediate SRAM cache (direct PE-to-DRAM communication), calculate the total DRAM reads required for matrices X and W using an Input Stationar
common_mistake: Candidates often assume the total DRAM read volume is simply the sum of the matrix sizes (16 KB + 512 KB = 528 KB) for both dataflows, forgetting that the stationary choice dictates how many times the non-stationary matrix must be re-fetched when tiling is required.
realistic_solution: In an Input Stationary dataflow, the entire 16 KB activation matrix X fits into the PE array and is pinned once. The 512 KB weight matrix W is streamed through. This results in 528 KB of total DRAM reads. In a Weight Stationary dataflow, W must be divided into 32 tiles of 16 KB. For each pinned W tile, the entire X matrix must be re-streamed from DRAM. This results in 1024 KB of total reads (512 KB for W + 32 * 16 KB for X). Therefore, Input Stationary is optimal for this shape.
napkin_math:
1. Data Sizes (INT8 = 1 byte):
   X size = 128 * 128 = 16,384 bytes = 16 KB
   W size = 128 * 4096 = 524,288 bytes = 512 KB

2. Input Stationary (IS) Reads:
   Pin X in PEs (fits perfectly): Read X once = 16 KB
   Stream W through PEs: Read W once = 512 KB
   Total IS Reads = 16 + 512 = 528 KB

3. Weight Stationary (WS) Reads:
   Pin W in PEs: W requires 32 tiles (512 KB / 16 KB)
   Read W once (across all tiles) = 512 KB
   Stream X for each tile: 32 passes * 16 KB = 512 KB
   Total WS Reads = 512 + 512 = 1,024 KB

Result: IS saves 496 KB of DRAM reads per inference compared to WS.

title: Architectural Trade-offs in Data Integration
scenario: Your team is evaluating two custom AI accelerator architectures for a massive DLRM (Deep Learning Recommendation Model) inference fleet processing terabytes of sparse embeddings. Option A features 2 PFLOPS of peak compute with a standard 2 TB/s HBM2e memory subsystem. Option B provides only 500 TFLOPS of peak compute but utilizes a novel 3D-stacked packaging approach delivering 10 TB/s of memory integration bandwidth at 5x lower energy-per-bit for data movement. Evaluate which architecture minimizes TCO and tail latency, justifying your choice quantitatively.
common_mistake: Candidates often overvalue peak compute (PFLOPS) and assume Option A will yield higher throughput, completely ignoring the low arithmetic intensity of DLRMs and the massive energy tax of the integration bottleneck.
realistic_solution: Select Option B. DLRMs are fundamentally memory-bandwidth bound due to extremely low arithmetic intensity (~1-2 FLOPs/byte). The energy and latency costs of moving massive embedding tables to parallel compute units dictate performance, making Option B's superior bandwidth and lower data-movement energy the optimal TCO choice.
napkin_math: Assume DLRM arithmetic intensity is 2 FLOPs/Byte. Option A throughput limit: 2 TB/s * 2 FLOPs/Byte = 4 TFLOPS (utilizing just 0.2% of its 2 PFLOPS). Option B throughput limit: 10 TB/s * 2 FLOPs/Byte = 20 TFLOPS. Option B delivers 5x the real-world throughput. Off-chip memory access costs ~15-20 pJ/bit vs <0.1 pJ/bit for compute, meaning Option B's 5x lower energy-per-bit dramatically reduces the primary power draw.

title: Diagnosing HBM-to-SRAM Integration Bottlenecks in Attention
scenario: You are deploying a large Transformer model on an NVIDIA H100 GPU (80GB HBM3, 3.35 TB/s bandwidth). Profiling reveals that standard Attention kernels are severely underutilizing the 989 TFLOPs (FP16) Tensor Cores. Furthermore, power consumption spikes during data transfers between HBM and SRAM rather than during matrix multiplications. How do you analyze this integration bottleneck, and what algorithmic or architectural optimization is required to keep the compute units fed?
common_mistake: Candidates often suggest simply increasing the batch size, buying faster HBM, or pruning the model, missing that the fundamental issue is the O(N^2) memory read/write complexity of standard attention materializing intermediate logits in HBM, which starves the compute units.
realistic_solution: Implement an IO-aware kernel like FlashAttention that fuses the attention operations. By tiling the Q, K, and V matrices and computing the softmax incrementally in the GPU's SRAM (50MB capacity), you bypass the HBM read/write integration bottleneck. This reduces memory accesses from O(N^2) to O(N) and allows Tensor Cores to run continuously without stalling for data.
napkin_math: Standard attention (N=4096, d=128): Intermediate attention matrix S = Q*K^T is 4096x4096. Storing S in FP16 takes 4096*4096*2 = 33.5 MB. Reading/writing this to HBM for softmax and V-multiplication requires ~134 MB of HBM traffic per head. Fusing operations and keeping this in SRAM saves ~134 MB of HBM bandwidth per head. Across 32 heads, this saves ~4.2 GB of HBM traffic per layer, bypassing the integration bottleneck.

title: Quantifying the Integration Bottleneck
scenario: You are deploying a 7-billion parameter auto-regressive language model (FP16) on an accelerator with 1.5 TB/s memory bandwidth and 300 TFLOPS of compute capacity. For single-token generation (batch size = 1), assume HBM memory access costs 40 pJ/byte and compute costs 0.1 pJ/FLOP. Calculate the time and energy ratios between moving the model weights and executing the computation to determine the primary bottleneck.
common_mistake: Focusing purely on the theoretical TFLOPS to estimate latency, entirely ignoring that auto-regressive decoding at low batch sizes is bounded by memory bandwidth and data movement energy, not arithmetic throughput.
realistic_solution: Calculate the exact data transfer time and energy versus compute time and energy to prove the workload is massively memory-bound. Then, propose solutions like increasing batch size to amortize weight loading, or using weight quantization (e.g., INT4) to reduce the integration bottleneck.
napkin_math:
Weights = 7B * 2 Bytes = 14 GB.
Transfer Time = 14 GB / 1.5 TB/s ≈ 9.33 ms.
Compute Time = (2 * 7B FLOPs) / 300 TFLOPS ≈ 0.046 ms.
Time Ratio: 9.33 / 0.046 ≈ 200x slower for data movement.
Transfer Energy = 14 GB * 40 pJ/Byte = 560 mJ.
Compute Energy = 14 GFLOPs * 0.1 pJ/FLOP = 1.4 mJ.
Energy Ratio: 560 / 1.4 = 400x more energy for data movement.

title: Team Draft Interleaving Click Attribution
scenario: A streaming platform runs an interleaved evaluation for a 4-item recommendation carousel. Model A (Control) ranks candidates as `[V1, V2, V3, V4, V5]`. Model B (Variant) ranks them as `[V2, V5, V1, V6, V7]`. You must apply the Team Draft Interleaving (TDI) algorithm to generate the carousel. In Round 1, Model A wins the coin toss to pick first. In Round 2, Model B wins the coin toss. Determine the exact 4-item interleaved list and the team attribution for each slot. If a user eventually clicks on `V2` and `V5`, calculate the final click-preference score for Model A vs Model B.
common_mistake: Assigning credit for the `V2` click to both Model A and Model B because both ranked it in their top 2, rather than strictly attributing the click to the model that drafted the item into the interleaved list.
realistic_solution: The interleaved list is `[V1 (from A), V2 (from B), V5 (from B), V3 (from A)]`. Since both clicked items (`V2` and `V5`) were drafted by Model B during the interleaving process, the final preference score is A=0, B=2.
napkin_math:
Round 1 (A wins toss): A drafts V1 (unselected). B drafts V2 (unselected). List: [V1_A, V2_B].
Round 2 (B wins toss): B drafts V5 (V2 is already taken). A drafts V3 (V1, V2 are already taken). List: [V1_A, V2_B, V5_B, V3_A].
Clicks: User clicks V2 (B's draft) and V5 (B's draft). Model A gets 0, Model B gets 2.

title: Diagnosing Attribution Collapse in Interleaved RecSys
scenario: An e-commerce platform is evaluating a new embedding-based retrieval model against a legacy BM25 model. In standard A/B testing (100k users), the neural model shows a +1.5% conversion lift. However, in a Team Draft Interleaving experiment combining both models' top-10 results into a single 20-item list, the neural model's attribution drops by 12% relative to BM25. Both models share 60% of their top-10 items. The interleaving API runs on a fleet of 50 c6i.2xlarge instances with a strict 40ms P99 latency budget. What is causing the discrepancy between the A/B test and the interleaving results?
common_mistake: Assuming the neural model suffers from latency degradation in production, or that the A/B test lacked statistical significance, missing the deduplication bias in the interleaving algorithm.
realistic_solution: Identify that the naive Team Draft Interleaving algorithm's deduplication logic is arbitrarily assigning credit for shared items to the BM25 model (if it drafts first or via coin-flip bias), starving the neural model of its highest-confidence predictions. The fix is to implement shared-credit attribution or use Probabilistic Interleaving.
napkin_math: In a Top-10 list with 60% overlap, there are 6 shared items and 4 unique items per model. If BM25 drafts first and claims the 6 shared items, BM25 effectively holds 10 items (6 shared + 4 unique) vs the Neural model's 4 unique items in the top slots. BM25 gets 2.5x more exposure for high-confidence predictions.

title: Mitigating P99 Jitter with Interrupt Shielding
scenario: You are serving a real-time fraud detection model on a multi-core server with 8x A100 GPUs and a 200Gbps NIC. At peak load (50,000 requests/sec), your median latency is a stable 2ms, but the P99 latency unpredictably spikes to 8-12ms. System profiles show that the CPU threads responsible for submitting CUDA kernels to the GPUs are experiencing frequent context switches and preemption, correlating directly with the arrival of high-throughput network traffic. Calculate the theoretical impact of NIC interrupts on kernel submission latency and determine the specific configuration changes needed to
common_mistake: Assuming the P99 spikes are caused by GPU saturation or Python GIL contention, leading to misguided efforts to increase batch sizes or aggressively quantize the model, which only worsens median latency without addressing the OS-level preemption.
realistic_solution: Disable the `irqbalance` daemon and manually configure `/proc/irq/default_smp_affinity` to route all NIC RX/TX hardware interrupts to a dedicated subset of CPU cores. Simultaneously, use `numactl` or `taskset` to pin the inference worker threads (handling GPU kernel submission) to a strictly disjoint set of cores on the same NUMA node, completely shielding them from interrupt handling.
napkin_math: At 50K RPS, the NIC generates at least 50K interrupts/sec (assuming minimal coalescing). If handling an interrupt and its associated context switch takes 50µs, the OS spends 2.5 CPU-seconds per second just on IRQs. If an IRQ preempts a thread about to launch a CUDA kernel, the thread suffers the 50µs handler delay plus CPU cache eviction/repopulation costs. With a queue depth of 10 requests waiting on that kernel, a single 100µs CPU-side delay translates to a 1ms+ queueing delay for downstream requests, compounding into the observed 8-12ms P99 spikes.

title: Diagnosing P99 Inference Jitter from NIC Interrupts
scenario: You are running a real-time speech-to-text inference service on an 8-GPU A100 server equipped with dual 100Gbps NICs and NVMe storage. Despite a steady GPU execution time of 8ms, your p99 latency sporadically spikes to 25-30ms during heavy network activity. Profiling shows CPU submission threads feeding the GPUs are occasionally delayed. How do you root-cause and resolve this latency jitter?
common_mistake: Focusing solely on CPU thread priorities (nice values) or thread pools without realizing that hardware interrupts (IRQs) from high-speed NICs bypass standard OS scheduling priorities and preempt application threads.
realistic_solution: Implement interrupt shielding. Pin GPU inference CPU threads to isolated cores (e.g., cores 16-63 via isolcpus), and configure IRQ affinity (smp_affinity) to route all NIC and NVMe hardware interrupts to dedicated housekeeping cores (e.g., cores 0-15).
napkin_math: A 100Gbps NIC handling 8M packets/sec with 50us interrupt coalescing generates 20,000 IRQs/sec. At 10us overhead per IRQ context switch, this steals 200ms of CPU time per second per core handling interrupts, directly preempting GPU launch threads and causing the 15-20ms latency spikes.

title: Interrupt Shielding vs NIC Coalescing for P99 Latency
scenario: You are deploying a high-throughput inference service on nodes equipped with 8x NVIDIA A100 GPUs, a 128-core CPU, and dual 200GbE NICs. While the average latency is 5ms, the P99 latency frequently spikes to 35ms, breaching the strict 20ms SLO. Profiling shows that CPU threads launching CUDA kernels are being unpredictably preempted by NIC hardware interrupts (SoftIRQs) distributed evenly across all cores by the OS. You must evaluate whether to implement strict CPU core isolation (interrupt shielding) or tune NIC interrupt coalescing to stabilize the P99 latency.
common_mistake: Candidates often suggest increasing NIC interrupt coalescing (batching packets before interrupting the CPU), failing to realize this directly increases the baseline average latency for all requests and doesn't solve the preemption of the CUDA launch threads.
realistic_solution: Implement interrupt shielding by pinning CUDA launch threads to specific cores (e.g., cores 16-127) using `taskset` or `cgroups`, and restricting NIC/NVMe interrupts to a disjoint set of dedicated non-inference cores (e.g., cores 0-15) using `/proc/irq/default_smp_affinity`.
napkin_math: At 200,000 packets/sec spread across 16 NIC queues, a core handles ~12,500 interrupts/sec. Even if an interrupt handler only takes 3µs (37.5ms/sec total CPU time), preempting a CUDA launch thread precisely when the GPU is idle can stall the GPU for the entire duration of the context switch, causing cascading queue build-ups and massive P99 spikes (10ms+).

title: Intersectional FRR Disparity in Facial Auth
scenario: You are auditing a cloud-based facial authentication API that processes 1M requests/day. The system reports an overall False Rejection Rate (FRR) of 1.2%. When evaluating demographic parity independently, the FRR for the 'Female' subgroup is 2.5%, and the FRR for the 'Darker Skin' subgroup is 3.1%. A customer complains about high failure rates for a specific demographic. Upon auditing a sample of 10,000 authentication attempts for the intersectional subgroup 'Darker-Skinned Females' (where 9,500 are legitimate users who should be accepted), you observe 3,296 false rejections. Calculate the int
common_mistake: Evaluating fairness only on independent axes (e.g., gender alone or skin tone alone) and assuming intersectional error rates are merely additive, completely missing compounded exponential bias.
realistic_solution: Evaluate error metrics across all overlapping identity combinations. For this system, the intersectional FRR is 34.7%, revealing a massive vulnerability that requires targeted intersectional data collection and loss function re-weighting.
napkin_math: Intersectional FRR = False Rejections / Expected Positives = 3,296 / 9,500 = 0.3469 (34.7%). Disparity Multiplier = Intersectional FRR / Overall FRR = 34.7% / 1.2% = 28.9x disparity.

title: Diagnosing Hidden Bias in Cloud KYC Pipelines
scenario: A cloud-based KYC (Know Your Customer) identity verification pipeline processes 100,000 onboarding requests daily. The global False Rejection Rate (FRR) is 1.2%. Single-attribute monitoring shows the FRR for female users is 2.3% and the FRR for users with Fitzpatrick skin types IV-VI (darker skin) is 4.0%. Despite passing the company's 5% single-axis SLA, customer support escalations reveal systemic failures for darker-skinned females, who make up 5% of the total request volume. Diagnostically, how do single-axis metrics mask this failure, and what is the theoretical maximum FRR for this speci
common_mistake: Relying strictly on marginal (single-axis) metrics like Demographic Parity or overall False Positive Rates, falsely assuming that if A and B are fair independently, the intersection of A and B is also fair.
realistic_solution: Implement intersectional subgroup evaluation (e.g., Min-Max Fairness evaluation across overlapping identity groups). Single-axis metrics mathematically dilute the severe errors of minority intersections by averaging them with larger, high-performing subgroups (e.g., lighter-skinned females or darker-skinned males).
napkin_math: Total requests = 100,000. Overall errors = 1,200 (1.2%). Assume Dark-skinned users = 20,000 (yielding 4.0% FRR = 800 errors). Assume Female users = 35,000 (yielding 2.3% FRR = 805 errors). Dark-skinned females = 5% of 100,000 = 5,000 users. If errors maximally overlap on this intersection, 800 of the dark-skinned errors could belong entirely to the 5,000 dark-skinned females. Maximum Subgroup FRR = 800 / 5,000 = 16.0%. The critical 16% error rate is completely masked by the larger 'Female' and 'Dark-skinned' denominator pools.

title: Evaluating Interleaving vs A/B Testing
scenario: Your e-commerce search platform processes 10,000 QPS. You are evaluating a new ranking model (Model B) that adds a cross-attention layer, increasing P99 latency by 45ms over the production Model A (currently at 80ms). Offline NDCG improved by 2.5%, but you need to detect a 0.5% CTR lift with 95% confidence. A traditional A/B test requires 14 days of traffic due to high inter-user variance. Evaluate an interleaving experiment design versus A/B testing, specifically addressing the statistical power, latency SLA (strict 150ms limit), and compute cost trade-offs.
common_mistake: Candidates blindly recommend interleaving for its statistical efficiency without realizing it requires running both Model A and Model B for the same request, pushing the P99 latency dangerously close to the 150ms SLA, and they forget interleaving cannot measure absolute metrics like total revenue.
realistic_solution: Use interleaving as a rapid, early-stage filter on a very small traffic allocation (e.g., 1%) to determine relative user preference in 24-48 hours. Run models in parallel to keep total latency at max(80ms, 125ms) + merge overhead = ~135ms, satisfying the 150ms SLA. If Model B wins, promote it to a standard A/B test to measure absolute CTR and revenue lift.
napkin_math: If inter-user variance accounts for 90% of total variance, interleaving (within-subject design) reduces required sample size by 10x, turning a 14-day test into 1.4 days. Latency: max(Latency A, Latency B) + Interleave Overhead = max(80, 125) + 10 = 135ms < 150ms SLA. Compute cost per interleaved request is A + B (100% increase).

title: Invariance Testing Compute Cost
scenario: You are deploying an automated loan approval model (latency: 100ms per request) processing 50,000 applications daily. To ensure fairness, you implement an invariance testing pipeline by substituting applicant names with 10 different demographic equivalents (as names act as proxy variables). If 2,500 applications change their final approval decision across any of their 10 permutations, calculate the total daily compute time added by this testing suite and the application-level invariance violation rate.
common_mistake: Calculating the violation rate based on the total number of permutations instead of the original applications, and ignoring the linear scaling of compute costs for perturbation tests.
realistic_solution: The pipeline adds 500,000 additional inferences, requiring 13.89 hours of sequential compute time (which should be batched). The invariance violation rate is calculated at the application level: 5%.
napkin_math: Additional inferences = 50,000 apps * 10 permutations = 500,000. Compute time = 500,000 * 100ms = 50,000 seconds = ~13.89 hours. Violation rate = 2,500 apps with flipped decisions / 50,000 total apps = 5%.

title: Root-Causing Variance in Resume Screening
scenario: You are debugging an NLP resume screening model deployed on T4 GPUs processing 10,000 resumes/hour. During an audit using invariance testing, you discover that swapping names associated with different demographics on otherwise identical resumes causes the 'Senior Engineer' confidence score to fluctuate between 62% and 85%. Furthermore, redacting the names entirely drops the score to 45%. Diagnose the root cause of this variance despite the redaction, and explain how to analyze the pipeline to fix it.
common_mistake: Assuming fairness through unawareness (redacting names) works, while failing to analyze how proxy variables (like specific university clubs or vocabulary choices) still leak demographic information into the dense embeddings.
realistic_solution: Analyze feature attributions (e.g., Integrated Gradients) on the mutated inputs to identify proxy variables. Apply Iterative Nullspace Projection (INLP) to project the 768-dimensional embeddings orthogonally to the demographic concept subspace before the classification head.
napkin_math: If the embedding dimension D=768 and the demographic concept subspace rank k=4, INLP projects embeddings to a (768-4)=764 dimensional nullspace. This O(D^2) projection per token takes ~1.2M FLOPs, adding negligible latency (<1ms on T4) while reducing demographic variance in scores by >95%.

title: Intersectional Bias Mitigation in Identity APIs
scenario: A cloud identity verification API processes 5M requests/day using a ResNet-101 model on NVIDIA T4 GPUs. The model achieves 99.2% overall accuracy and satisfies demographic parity across gender (99.0% vs 99.3%) and skin tone (98.9% vs 99.4%) independently. An audit reveals the false rejection rate (FRR) for darker-skinned females is 12.5%, compared to 0.5% for lighter-skinned males. You must evaluate remediation strategies, comparing the trade-offs between a 10x targeted data collection (delaying launch by 3 months) versus deploying a multi-headed Mixture of Experts architecture (increasing ser
common_mistake: Assuming that achieving demographic parity on individual marginal distributions (e.g., gender, race) mathematically guarantees fairness across their joint intersectional distributions.
realistic_solution: Implement a Minimax optimization objective during fine-tuning to explicitly minimize the loss of the worst-performing intersectional subgroup, accepting a slight degradation in overall accuracy (e.g., to 98.7%) to reduce the worst-case FRR below 2%, which avoids architecture changes that violate the 200ms serving SLO.
napkin_math: Assuming 1M training images with marginals P(Female)=0.5 and P(Dark)=0.5, independent probability P(Female ∩ Dark) = 0.25. If the actual joint distribution is skewed to P(Female ∩ Dark) = 0.04 (40k images), a batch size of 256 yields only ~10 samples/batch for this subgroup. Achieving the 50 samples/batch needed for stable gradients requires a 5x oversampling rate, introducing severe overfitting risk unless coupled with aggressive data augmentation or group-distributionally robust optimization (Group DRO).

title: Evaluating Invariance Testing Architectures at Scale
scenario: You are designing the evaluation pipeline for a cloud-based mortgage approval LLM (175B parameters) running on 8x H100 GPUs. The model evaluates a 4,000-token financial history. You must implement invariance testing to ensure altering protected attributes (or their proxies, like zip code) shifts the approval probability by less than 0.5%. Generating counterfactuals dynamically via another LLM adds 150ms latency per prompt and costs $0.002 per invocation. With a validation set of 100,000 applicants and 20 CI/CD runs per day, evaluate the trade-offs between dynamic on-the-fly counterfactual gene
common_mistake: Candidates often choose dynamic on-the-fly generation for maximum coverage without calculating the aggregate CI/CD costs, or they fail to recognize that dynamic LLM rewriting can inadvertently alter valid financial signals (semantic drift), invalidating the invariance test.
realistic_solution: Implement a static, pre-computed counterfactual dataset. Use a cheaper model or rule-based templates once to generate the perturbations, verify semantic consistency rigorously, and store them. Run the 175B model in offline batch mode (e.g., using vLLM) against the static pairs during CI/CD to guarantee exact reproducibility.
napkin_math: Dynamic cost: 100,000 queries * $0.002 = $200 per run. At 20 runs/day, cost is $4,000/day or ~$1.46M/year. Static generation is a one-time $200 cost. Dynamic generation latency: 15,000 seconds (4.1 hours) sequentially, requiring massive parallelization to fit into a 15-minute CI/CD SLA, which heavily taxes the cluster.

title: Calculating IO Overhead in Standard Attention
scenario: You are profiling a Transformer model on an A100 GPU (1.5 TB/s theoretical HBM bandwidth). The standard unoptimized self-attention implementation materializes the N x N attention logits (S) and probabilities (P) to HBM. For a batch size of 16, 16 attention heads, and sequence length of 8192 using FP16 (2 bytes/element), calculate the absolute time spent strictly on writing and reading these two intermediate matrices to and from HBM.
common_mistake: Focusing purely on the FLOPs required for the QK^T and PV matrix multiplications while ignoring the massive O(N^2) memory I/O cost of intermediate tensors.
realistic_solution: IO-aware algorithms like FlashAttention restructure the computation to tile blocks of Q, K, and V in SRAM, fusing the operations and entirely bypassing the need to read/write the full S and P matrices to HBM.
napkin_math: Matrix elements = 16 (batch) * 16 (heads) * 8192 * 8192 = 17.18 billion. Size per matrix = 17.18e9 * 2 bytes = ~34.36 GB. Standard attention requires: Write S, Read S, Write P, Read P. Total data movement = 4 * 34.36 GB = 137.4 GB. At 1.5 TB/s (1500 GB/s), memory I/O time = 137.4 / 1500 = ~91.6 ms per layer.

title: Evaluating IO-Aware Attention on A100
scenario: Your team is training a 7B parameter Transformer model on a cluster of NVIDIA A100 GPUs (40GB HBM2, 1.5 TB/s memory bandwidth, 312 TFLOPS BF16). Profiling the standard self-attention implementation reveals it is heavily memory-bound. For sequence length 4096, attention takes 10ms per layer. An engineer proposes a new attention algorithm that computes attention scores on-the-fly in SRAM (192KB per SM) without writing the N x N attention matrix to HBM. However, this algorithm requires recomputing values during the backward pass, increasing total FLOPs by 15%. Evaluate whether this proposed algor
common_mistake: Rejecting the proposal because it increases total FLOPs, failing to realize that for memory-bound operations, trading extra compute for reduced HBM IO yields a net speedup.
realistic_solution: Accepting the proposal. Standard attention writes an O(N^2) matrix to HBM. At N=4096, this IO time dwarfs compute time. The proposed IO-aware algorithm (like FlashAttention) recomputes the forward pass during the backward pass (increasing FLOPs) but avoids writing/reading the intermediate matrix to/from HBM, significantly reducing total runtime because the A100 has a massive FLOP-to-byte ratio.
napkin_math: Standard Attention HBM IO: Writing and reading an N=4096 matrix (16.7M elements per head, ~33MB in BF16). Multiplied by batch size 8 and 32 heads, this is ~8.5GB per layer just for the attention matrix. At 1.5 TB/s, moving 8.5GB takes ~5.6ms of pure transfer time. Since A100 has 312 TFLOPS, an extra 15% FLOPs on a compute block that is highly underutilized adds negligible compute overhead (e.g., <0.5ms), which is heavily outweighed by saving >5ms of IO bottlenecks.

title: Diagnosing Activation Function Memory Bottlenecks
scenario: A custom activation function `y = x1 * torch.sigmoid(x2)` is applied to two FP16 tensors `x1` and `x2`, each with 100 million elements (200MB each). Profiling on an A100 GPU (2 TB/s memory bandwidth) shows this simple operation takes ~500 microseconds. Since the compute required is less than 1 GFLOP, the team is confused why it's so slow compared to the 312 TFLOPs peak compute of the A100. Diagnose the root cause of this performance bottleneck.
common_mistake: Assuming the bottleneck is compute or Python-level overhead, and attempting to optimize it by simplifying the math operations, ignoring that element-wise operations are fundamentally bounded by memory bandwidth.
realistic_solution: Identify that the operation is severely memory-bound. Implement kernel fusion using Triton or `torch.compile` to load `x1` and `x2` into SRAM once, compute the sigmoid and multiplication, and write `y` back, eliminating intermediate HBM reads/writes.
napkin_math: Naive execution creates an intermediate tensor: `temp = torch.sigmoid(x2)` (Read 200MB, Write 200MB) + `y = x1 * temp` (Read 200MB x1, Read 200MB temp, Write 200MB y) = 1.0 GB total HBM I/O. At 2TB/s bandwidth, time = 1.0 GB / 2000 GB/s = 500 μs. Fused execution: Read x1 (200MB) + Read x2 (200MB) + Write y (200MB) = 600MB total HBM I/O. Time = 600MB / 2000 GB/s = 300 μs. Arithmetic intensity (fused) is ~5 FLOPs / 6 bytes ≈ 0.8 FLOPs/byte, far below the A100 ridge point (~156 FLOPs/byte).

title: Applying the Iron Law of ML Systems
scenario: Your ML serving platform handles a dense retrieval model. Profiling shows each request processes 10 GFLOPs of compute and reads 200 MB of data. Your current cloud GPU provides 20 TFLOPS of compute and 1 TB/s of memory bandwidth. The serving framework adds a fixed 300 µs of overhead per request. Using the Iron Law of ML Systems, evaluate which of the following three architectural investments will provide the largest absolute reduction in end-to-end latency: (A) Upgrading to a GPU with 2 TB/s memory bandwidth, (B) Applying model pruning to reduce compute to 5 GFLOPs, or (C) Rewriting the serving
common_mistake: Candidates often intuitively assume hardware upgrades (bandwidth or compute) are always the best optimization path without calculating the exact theoretical time spent in each phase of the Iron Law.
realistic_solution: Decompose the latency using the Iron Law: T_total = T_data + T_compute + T_overhead. Calculate current times: T_data = 200 MB / 1 TB/s = 200 µs. T_compute = 10 GFLOPs / 20 TFLOPS = 500 µs. T_overhead = 300 µs. Option C (framework rewrite) saves 280 µs, outperforming Option B (250 µs savings) and Option A (100 µs savings).
napkin_math:
Base Latency = (200e6 / 1e12) + (10e9 / 20e12) + 300µs = 200µs + 500µs + 300µs = 1000µs.
(A) BW to 2 TB/s: T_data drops to 100µs (saves 100µs).
(B) Prune to 5 GFLOPs: T_comp drops to 250µs (saves 250µs).
(C) Overhead to 20µs: T_over drops to 20µs (saves 280µs).
Conclusion: Option C provides the maximum latency reduction.

title: LLM Inference Bottleneck Analysis
scenario: You are profiling a 7B parameter LLM inference service on a single NVIDIA A100 (1.5 TB/s memory bandwidth, 312 TFLOPs BF16). For batch size 1 (generating one token at a time), the observed latency per token is 10ms. The ML team proposes pruning 50% of the model's linear layer weights to cut the compute FLOPs in half, expecting a massive latency drop. Analyze why this compute-focused optimization will fail to significantly improve single-batch latency using the Iron Law of ML Systems.
common_mistake: Assuming that reducing total mathematical operations (FLOPs) will proportionally reduce latency, failing to recognize that single-batch autoregressive generation is heavily memory bandwidth bound, not compute-bound.
realistic_solution: Decompose the latency into data movement, compute, and overhead. Identify that loading 14GB of weights from HBM dominates the latency, while compute time is negligible. Explain that optimization must target memory bandwidth (e.g., quantization, speculative decoding) rather than just FLOPs.
napkin_math: Weights: 7B * 2 bytes = 14 GB. Data movement time: 14 GB / 1500 GB/s = 9.33ms. Compute FLOPs: ~14 GFLOPs. Compute time: 14 GFLOPs / 312,000 GFLOPs = 0.04ms. Compute is < 0.5% of total time. Cutting FLOPs by 50% only saves 0.02ms.

title: Calculating Single-Token Latency on A100
scenario: You are deploying a 7 billion parameter LLM for single-token generation (batch size 1) on a single NVIDIA A100 80GB GPU. The A100 provides a peak BF16 compute throughput of 312 TFLOPS and an HBM2e memory bandwidth of 2039 GB/s. Both weights and activations use BF16 precision. Assume the system has a fixed kernel launch and framework overhead of 1.5 ms per forward pass. Calculate the expected latency for generating a single token and identify the primary bottleneck using the Iron Law of ML Systems.
common_mistake: Calculating only the compute time (Ops / Peak FLOPs) while ignoring the time required to load the model weights from HBM into the SM registers, leading to a massive underestimation of latency.
realistic_solution: Apply the Iron Law of ML Systems: Total Latency = (Data Volume / Bandwidth) + (Operations / Peak Compute) + Fixed Overhead. First, calculate the data volume: 7B params * 2 bytes = 14 GB. Memory time = 14 GB / 2039 GB/s ≈ 6.86 ms. Next, calculate compute time: 2 Ops/param * 7B = 14 GFLOPs. Compute time = 14 GFLOPs / 312,000 GFLOPs ≈ 0.04 ms. Finally, add the 1.5 ms overhead. The total latency is ~8.4 ms, and the generation process is profoundly memory bandwidth bound.
napkin_math:
Time = T_memory + T_compute + T_overhead
T_memory = (7e9 * 2 bytes) / (2039e9 bytes/s) = 6.86 ms
T_compute = (7e9 * 2 FLOPs) / (312e12 FLOPs/s) = 0.04 ms
T_overhead = 1.5 ms
Total T = 6.86 + 0.04 + 1.5 = 8.4 ms

title: Evaluating Topologies via the Iron Law
scenario: You are planning to train a 70B parameter dense LLM on 1.4 trillion tokens using a cluster of 512 A100 (80GB) GPUs. Your infrastructure team presents two cluster leasing options. Option A uses standard RoCEv2 Ethernet networking, projecting a Model FLOPs Utilization (MFU) of 35% at a cost of $2.00/GPU-hour. Option B uses non-blocking InfiniBand networking, projecting an MFU of 50% at a cost of $2.80/GPU-hour. Using the Iron Law of Training, evaluate which option is more cost-effective for this specific training run, and determine the exact breakeven MFU for Option B that would make the costs i
common_mistake: Candidates often try to calculate the absolute training time in seconds by computing the total FLOPs (6PD = 5.88e23) and the system's peak throughput (512 * 312 TFLOPs), introducing arithmetic errors, rather than realizing that workload and peak hardware are constants that can be factored out.
realistic_solution: Recognize that Total Cost is proportional to Training Time * GPU_Hourly_Cost. According to the Iron Law of Training, Time = Total_FLOPs / (System_Peak_FLOPs * MFU). Since Total_FLOPs and System_Peak_FLOPs are identical for both options, Time is strictly proportional to 1/MFU. We can compare the cost effectiveness by calculating the ratio: (GPU_Hourly_Cost / MFU). Option B has a lower ratio (5.60 vs 5.71), making it cheaper overall despite the higher hourly rate.
napkin_math:
Cost Factor A = $2.00 / 0.35 = 5.71
Cost Factor B = $2.80 / 0.50 = 5.60
Since 5.60 < 5.71, Option B (InfiniBand) is cheaper overall.
Breakeven MFU_B = $2.80 * (0.35 / $2.00) = 0.49 (49% MFU).

title: Analyzing True MFU vs GPU Utilization
scenario: You are training a 10B parameter transformer model on 1 Trillion tokens using a cluster of 256 NVIDIA A100 GPUs (each with 312 TFLOPs peak BF16). The infrastructure team reports that the training run will take 35 days and notes that `nvidia-smi` consistently shows 95% GPU utilization, concluding that the cluster is highly optimized. Use the Iron Law of Training to analyze the true efficiency. What is the actual bottleneck?
common_mistake: Conflating `nvidia-smi` GPU utilization with Model FLOPs Utilization (MFU). Candidates often fail to realize that `nvidia-smi` only measures whether the SM is active, not whether it is doing useful math.
realistic_solution: Calculate the true MFU using the Iron Law of Training. The MFU is only 25%. A high SM utilization alongside low MFU indicates GPUs are active but stalled on memory bandwidth, un-fused kernels, or fine-grained communication.
napkin_math: Total Ops ≈ 6ND = 6 * 10B params * 1T tokens = 60 ZFLOPs. Peak Compute = 256 * 312 TFLOPs ≈ 80 PFLOPs. Time = 35 days ≈ 3,000,000 seconds. Actual Throughput = 60 ZFLOPs / 3M sec = 20 PFLOPs. True MFU = 20 PFLOPs / 80 PFLOPs = 25%.

title: Shift-Left Constraint Validation Savings
scenario: Your ML team trains a recommendation model on a node with 8x A100 GPUs. Full training takes 50 hours. Currently, latency and memory constraints for the target serving environment are only evaluated after training completes. Historically, 50% of trained models fail these late-stage checks and are discarded. You propose a 'shift-left' validation step that tests constraints on a randomly initialized dummy model before training begins, taking just 0.5 hours on the 8x A100 node. Calculate the expected savings in A100 GPU-hours per successfully deployed model.
common_mistake: Calculating savings for a single run instead of calculating the expected savings per successful deployment, which must account for the 50% failure rate requiring 2 runs per success on average.
realistic_solution: With a 50% failure rate, every successful deployment is accompanied by exactly 1 failed run on average. By catching the failure in 0.5 hours instead of 50 hours, you save 49.5 node hours of cluster time per failed run.
napkin_math: Runs per success = 1 / (1 - 0.5) = 2 (1 success, 1 failure). Current wasted time per success = 1 failure * 50 hrs * 8 GPUs = 400 GPU-hours. New wasted time per success = 1 failure * 0.5 hrs * 8 GPUs = 4 GPU-hours. Savings = 400 - 4 = 396 A100 GPU-hours per successful model.

title: Diagnosing High Iteration Tax in RTB Models
scenario: A cloud ML team is developing a real-time bidding model. The model trains for 72 hours on A100 GPUs and is then deployed to T4 instances for staging validation. Over the past three months, 60% of models that completed training failed staging because their p99 latency exceeded the strict 15ms budget on the target T4s. The team then prunes the architecture and retrains. Each failed iteration wastes 4 days of wall-clock time and substantial compute budget. Analyze the root cause of this pipeline inefficiency and propose a structural fix.
common_mistake: Focusing on accelerating the 72-hour training job by scaling to more GPUs, or relaxing inference constraints by upgrading staging to A100s, which treats the symptom rather than the delayed feedback loop.
realistic_solution: Shift the constraint verification left. Compile the randomly initialized model architecture for the target T4 hardware and profile its latency before training begins. Abort the pipeline immediately if the 15ms budget is exceeded.
napkin_math: Current Iteration Tax = 72h (train) + 24h (deploy) = 96h per failure. With a 60% failure rate, an average of 57.6h is wasted per attempt. Pre-training latency profiling takes ~5 mins (0.08h). Time saved per architecture failure = 95.9 hours.

title: Evaluating Iteration Tax in LLM Deployment
scenario: A cloud AI team is developing a 70B parameter LLM for real-time customer support using 8x H100 (80GB) GPU nodes. The data science team trains for 4 weeks before handing off to the ML engineering team, who discovers the model requires 140GB of KV cache memory for the target batch size of 256, exceeding the available memory when partitioned across 2 GPUs. They must redesign the architecture to use 4-way tensor parallelism and Grouped Query Attention (GQA), requiring a full retraining cycle. Evaluate the cost of this iteration tax and propose a rigorous workflow change to prevent it.
common_mistake: Treating training and deployment as isolated phases, assuming that if a model trains successfully, it can be served efficiently, ignoring the strict memory and latency constraints of the target production environment.
realistic_solution: Implement a 'shift-left' constraint propagation strategy. Before training, profile a dummy model with the target architecture (70B) and batch size (256) on the exact target hardware (H100) to validate KV cache and serving latency constraints.
napkin_math: Wasted compute = 4 weeks * 7 days * 24 hours * 8 GPUs * $3/hr = $16,128. Opportunity cost = 4 weeks of delayed launch. Total turnaround time becomes 4 (initial) + 1 (debug) + 4 (retrain) = 9 weeks. A 1-day pre-training profiling step saves 4 weeks and $16k.

title: Calculating JSD for Disjoint Distributions
scenario: You are deploying an automated drift detection system for a categorical feature with two states: 'A' and 'B'. The reference training distribution P is entirely 'A' (P = [1.0, 0.0]). Due to a downstream pipeline bug, the incoming production distribution Q shifts entirely to 'B' (Q = [0.0, 1.0]). A junior engineer attempts to use Kullback-Leibler (KL) divergence to trigger an alert, but the metric calculation crashes the monitoring service. Explain why it crashes, and calculate the exact Jensen-Shannon Divergence (JSD) using base-2 logarithms to determine the proper bounded metric value.
common_mistake: Failing to realize that KL divergence is asymmetric and undefined when Q(i) = 0 and P(i) > 0, leading to a division by zero error in production.
realistic_solution: Use Jensen-Shannon Divergence, which creates a smoothed midpoint distribution M = (P+Q)/2. This ensures no division by zero and bounds the drift metric between 0 and 1 (when using base-2 logs), making it ideal for absolute alerting thresholds.

title: Diagnosing Infinite Drift Alerts in KL Divergence
scenario: You are managing an anomaly detection model serving 5,000 requests per second on AWS Inferentia. The pipeline runs an hourly data drift check comparing the live feature distributions (Q) against the training baseline (P) using Kullback-Leibler (KL) divergence. Recently, the operations team reported severe alert fatigue: the KL divergence metric frequently spikes to near infinity during certain hours, triggering PagerDuty incidents, even though the overall model accuracy remains stable at 94%. Upon investigation, you find that the live data occasionally contains rare categorical feature values
common_mistake: Candidates often suggest applying arbitrary clipping to the KL divergence scores or manually filtering out out-of-vocabulary categories, which masks genuine drift and requires constant manual threshold tuning.
realistic_solution: Diagnose that KL divergence is asymmetric and unbounded, spiking to infinity when P(x) is zero for a rare event in the baseline. Replace KL divergence with Jensen-Shannon (JS) divergence, which calculates the symmetric KL divergence to a mixture distribution M = 0.5 * (P + Q). JS is bounded between 0 and 1, providing stable, normalized thresholds for alarming.
napkin_math: If Q has a rare event where P(x) = 0 and Q(x) = 0.01: KL(Q||P) contains the term Q(x) * log(Q(x)/P(x)) = 0.01 * log(0.01/0) -> ∞. For JS divergence: M(x) = 0.5 * (0 + 0.01) = 0.005. The bounded term in KL(Q||M) becomes Q(x) * log(Q(x)/M(x)) = 0.01 * log(0.01/0.005) = 0.01 * log(2) = 0.01. Overall JS divergence is strictly bounded to max 1 bit.

title: Drift Detection Thresholds in High-Throughput Recommendation Systems
scenario: You are designing an automated drift detection pipeline for a recommendation system serving 50,000 requests per second on a fleet of AWS g5.12xlarge instances. The system monitors the distribution of 256-dimensional user embeddings over 1-hour windows (approx 180M requests) against a reference training distribution. You must select a distance metric to trigger automated retraining pipelines. Evaluate the trade-offs between Kullback-Leibler (KL) Divergence and Jensen-Shannon (JS) Divergence for setting hard alerting thresholds in this high-throughput, dynamic environment.
common_mistake: Selecting KL Divergence and attempting to handle its unboundedness and asymmetry with complex, heuristic clipping. Candidates often overlook that KL(P||Q) explodes to infinity if the production distribution (P) encounters novel feature values not present in the reference distribution (Q).
realistic_solution: Adopt Jensen-Shannon Divergence (JSD) for automated alerting. JSD is symmetric and bounded between 0 and 1 (when using base-2 logarithms), making it vastly superior for setting stable, universal thresholds (e.g., triggering retraining at JSD > 0.15) without crashing or firing false positives when encountering out-of-vocabulary data.
napkin_math: If a novel categorical feature appears in prod with frequency 0.01, reference Q=0. KL(P||Q) = 0.01 * log(0.01 / 0) → ∞. For JSD, M = (P+Q)/2 = 0.005. JSD contributes 0.5 * 0.01 * log(0.01 / 0.005) = 0.005 * 1 = 0.005 to the divergence score, remaining perfectly stable.

title: JIT Recompilation Spikes in Dynamic Inference
scenario: You are running a production NLP inference service on NVIDIA A100 GPUs. To improve throughput, your team enabled JIT compilation (e.g., `torch.compile` or XLA). While median latency improved by 30%, P99 latency severely degraded, spiking from 45ms to over 2.5 seconds. The incoming API requests have highly variable sequence lengths ranging from 10 to 1024 tokens. How do you root-cause and resolve these P99 latency spikes?
common_mistake: Failing to recognize that variable input shapes trigger JIT recompilation. Candidates often incorrectly blame GPU memory fragmentation, Python garbage collection, or network I/O, missing the graph compiler's caching mechanics.
realistic_solution: Diagnose that the JIT compiler generates a new execution graph for every unique sequence length, causing cache misses and 2.5s recompilations during inference. Fix this by padding inputs to power-of-two buckets (e.g., 128, 256, 512, 1024) to constrain the shape space, or by explicitly enabling dynamic shapes in the JIT compiler.
napkin_math: Without JIT: P99 = 45ms. With JIT (unbounded shapes): Cache hit = 30ms, Cache miss = 2500ms. If 2% of requests have a previously unseen sequence length, P99 is dominated by the 2500ms recompilation penalty. Bucketing reduces the state space to 4 shapes; after 4 initial compilations (10s total), cache hit rate reaches 100%, stabilizing P99 at ~35ms (including padding overhead).

title: Quantifying Element-wise Kernel Fusion Speedup
scenario: You are profiling a custom activation layer `y = x * torch.sigmoid(x) + bias` applied to 1GB FP16 tensors (`x` and `bias`) on an NVIDIA A100 GPU (2 TB/s memory bandwidth). The layer is currently running in PyTorch eager mode without `torch.compile`. Calculate the theoretical performance speedup you would achieve by writing a custom CUDA kernel that fuses these three operations into a single pass.
common_mistake: Focusing on FLOP reductions or assuming speedup is directly proportional to the number of fused operations (e.g., 3 ops = 3x speedup). Candidates often forget to tally the exact HBM reads and writes for intermediate tensors.
realistic_solution: Count total memory traffic for both approaches. Element-wise ops are deeply memory-bound, so execution time is proportional to HBM bytes transferred. Eager mode materializes two intermediate tensors, resulting in 8GB of traffic. Fusing the operations keeps intermediates in registers, dropping traffic to 3GB.
napkin_math:
Unfused traffic: t1=sigmoid(x) [Read 1GB, Write 1GB = 2GB] -> t2=x*t1 [Read 2GB, Write 1GB = 3GB] -> y=t2+bias [Read 2GB, Write 1GB = 3GB]. Total = 8GB.
Fused traffic: Read x (1GB), Read bias (1GB), Write y (1GB). Total = 3GB.
Speedup = Unfused Traffic / Fused Traffic = 8GB / 3GB = ~2.67x.

title: Calculating JIT Kernel Fusion Latency
scenario: You are optimizing a PyTorch-based Transformer inference service running on NVIDIA A10G GPUs (125 TFLOPs FP16). Under eager execution, a single inference request takes 15.0ms. Profiling reveals that 8.0ms is spent on CPU-side Python framework dispatch overhead, while actual GPU kernel execution takes 7.0ms. You apply `torch.compile` to JIT-compile the model. If the JIT trace completely eliminates the Python dispatch overhead and kernel fusion (e.g., fusing LayerNorm and GeLU) reduces the GPU execution time by 20%, calculate the new inference latency and the total latency reduction achieved aft
common_mistake: Assuming JIT compilation only speeds up the GPU execution time via kernel fusion, while forgetting that its primary benefit in eager frameworks is bypassing the CPU-bound Python dispatch overhead.
realistic_solution: The new inference latency drops to 5.6ms, achieving a total reduction of 9.4ms. JIT compilation removes the 8.0ms CPU dispatch overhead entirely and shrinks the 7.0ms GPU execution time to 5.6ms via kernel fusion.
napkin_math: Eager Latency = 8.0ms (CPU) + 7.0ms (GPU) = 15.0ms. JIT Latency = 0ms (CPU dispatch bypassed) + (7.0ms * 0.8) (GPU fused) = 5.6ms. Latency Reduction = 15.0ms - 5.6ms = 9.4ms.

title: Root-Causing Memory-Bound Activations
scenario: While profiling a PyTorch recommendation model on an NVIDIA A100 (2 TB/s HBM bandwidth), you find that a custom element-wise activation block `y = x * torch.tanh(a * x + b)` (where 'a' and 'b' are learned scalars) operating on a 250MB activation tensor takes roughly 1.1 milliseconds. Nsight Systems reveals 5% SM utilization but 95% HBM bandwidth utilization. How do you analyze this bottleneck, and what is the structural cause within the framework?
common_mistake: Assuming the bottleneck is compute-related and trying to optimize the floating-point math, or failing to account for the implicit intermediate tensor allocations caused by eager-mode framework execution.
realistic_solution: Diagnose this as a lack of kernel fusion. Eager-mode PyTorch executes this as four distinct operations sequentially. Analyze the profiler trace to see four separate kernels, each reading and writing intermediate tensors to HBM. Resolve this by using `torch.compile()` or writing a custom Triton kernel to fuse all element-wise operations into a single pass, eliminating intermediate memory materialization.
napkin_math: Unfused traffic: `a*x` (1R, 1W), `+b` (1R, 1W), `tanh` (1R, 1W), `x*` (2R, 1W). Total = 5 Reads (1.25GB) + 4 Writes (1.0GB) = 2.25GB. At 2 TB/s, ideal time = 2.25GB / 2TB/s = 1.125 ms. Fused traffic: 1 Read (x), 1 Write (y) = 0.5GB total. Ideal time = 0.5GB / 2TB/s = 250 µs. Fusing eliminates 1.75GB of unnecessary HBM traffic, yielding a 4.5x theoretical speedup.

title: Mitigating Dispatch Bottlenecks with CUDA Graphs
scenario: You are profiling a real-time recommendation model running on an NVIDIA A100. The model executes a sequence of 400 small, independent multi-layer perceptrons (MLPs) per request. Profiling shows that each MLP takes only 2 µs to execute on the device, but the GPU utilization is hovering around 28%. Given a typical CUDA kernel launch overhead of 5 µs per kernel, calculate the total execution time for these 400 MLPs and demonstrate how to optimize this dispatch bottleneck.
common_mistake: Candidates calculate the theoretical compute time (400 * 2 µs = 0.8 ms) and assume the GPU is underpowered or memory-bound, attempting to optimize the math kernels rather than recognizing the CPU dispatch overhead.
realistic_solution: Identify that the system is CPU dispatch-bound. Implement CUDA Graphs to capture the entire sequence of 400 kernel launches into a single executable graph, amortizing the launch overhead.
napkin_math: Standard dispatch: 400 kernels * (5 µs launch + 2 µs compute) = 2.8 ms total latency (GPU is active for 0.8 ms, yielding ~28% utilization). CUDA Graphs dispatch: 1 graph launch (~3 µs) + (400 * 2 µs compute) = 0.803 ms total latency. Speedup: ~3.5x.

title: Evaluating JIT Compilation for Dynamic Shapes
scenario: Your team is deploying a Transformer-based NLP inference service on NVIDIA A10G GPUs. The model handles highly dynamic user queries with sequence lengths ranging from 10 to 512 tokens. In eager mode, the average inference latency is 15ms per request. To meet a strict 5ms P99 latency SLA, an engineer proposes wrapping the model in a JIT compiler (e.g., `torch.compile`). However, during load testing, the service experiences severe latency spikes of up to 500ms for almost every new request. Evaluate the architectural trade-offs of this JIT approach for dynamic workloads and propose a quantitative
common_mistake: Assuming JIT compilation always provides an unconditional speedup without accounting for recompilation overhead. Candidates often miss that tracing JIT compilers generate a new optimized graph for every unique input shape by default, turning inference into a compilation bottleneck.
realistic_solution: Implement input sequence bucketization (e.g., padding to nearest powers of 2: 16, 32, 64, 128, 256, 512) alongside enabling compiler dynamic shape support. This restricts the total possible compiled graphs to just 6, amortizing the heavy compilation overhead while achieving the target optimized inference latency.
napkin_math: Eager mode (1,000 requests): 1,000 * 15ms = 15,000ms total. Naive JIT (worst case, 500 unique lengths): 500 * 500ms (compile) + 1,000 * 4ms (infer) = 254,000ms. Bucketized JIT (6 buckets): 6 * 500ms (compile) + 1,000 * 4ms (infer) = 3,000ms + 4,000ms = 7,000ms (over 2x faster than eager, with amortized P99 latency dropping to 4ms after warmup).

title: Evaluating Fusion Strategies for Custom Layers
scenario: Your team is optimizing a custom Transformer layer on A100 GPUs (2039 GB/s HBM bandwidth). The layer includes a sequence of memory-bound operations: an element-wise activation, a custom normalization, and a masking step. Unfused, these operations process a 50MB tensor and take 150us. You must evaluate three options: (1) keep them as separate PyTorch operations, (2) write a hand-tuned CUDA kernel, or (3) use a JIT compiler like Triton. Justify your decision comparing memory traffic, execution latency, and engineering overhead.
common_mistake: Assuming custom CUDA is always required for optimal performance, ignoring the significant engineering debt, or failing to identify that the operations are purely memory bandwidth bound rather than compute bound.
realistic_solution: Use a JIT compiler like Triton (e.g., via torch.compile) to automatically fuse the operations. Since these are memory-bound, the primary latency comes from HBM reads/writes of intermediate tensors. Fusion keeps intermediates in SRAM. Triton achieves near-native CUDA performance for element-wise and reduction fusions with minimal maintenance overhead.
napkin_math: Unfused: 3 operations require reading and writing the 50MB tensor (3 ops * 2 transfers * 50MB = 300MB total traffic). At 2 TB/s HBM bandwidth, bandwidth-limited time is 300MB / 2000GB/s = 150us. Fused: Read input once, write output once (2 transfers * 50MB = 100MB traffic). Bandwidth-limited time drops to 100MB / 2000GB/s = 50us. You achieve a 3x speedup purely from bandwidth savings.

title: Diagnosing CPU Bottlenecks in Batch-1 Inference
scenario: A team is serving a large 70B parameter transformer model on 4x A100 GPUs using PyTorch. For batch size 32, GPU utilization is 90% and throughput is excellent. However, for real-time batch size 1 requests, the service misses its 30ms latency SLA, averaging 45ms. Profiling reveals the GPUs are idle 60% of the time, while the CPU thread running the PyTorch event loop is pegged at 100%. The model execution trace shows thousands of small sequential operations like LayerNorm, GeLU, and element-wise additions.
common_mistake: Assuming the issue is memory bandwidth (since batch size 1 is often memory bound) or compute latency, and attempting to fix it with tensor parallelism or weight quantization while ignoring the CPU-side bottleneck.
realistic_solution: Identify that the CPU cannot dispatch kernels fast enough to keep the GPU fed (kernel launch overhead). Apply CUDA Graphs or torch.compile to trace the operations and replay them with a single CPU launch, or use fused kernels (e.g., FlashAttention, fused MLP) to drastically reduce the total number of kernel launches.
napkin_math: If the model has 2,500 distinct operations and each CPU-to-GPU kernel launch takes ~10µs, the total launch overhead alone is 2,500 * 10µs = 25ms. If the actual GPU execution takes 15ms, the total latency is 40ms, meaning over 60% of the time is just CPU dispatch overhead.

title: Calculating KL Divergence for Feature Drift
scenario: A cloud recommender system expects an age group feature distribution of P = {18-25: 0.5, 26-35: 0.3, 36+: 0.2}. Recent production traffic Q has shifted to {18-25: 0.2, 26-35: 0.5, 36+: 0.3}. The monitoring system is configured to fire a severity-1 alert if the drift distance exceeds 0.20 nats. Calculate the KL Divergence D_KL(P||Q) to determine if the alert should fire.
common_mistake: Calculating D_KL(Q||P) instead of D_KL(P||Q), or assuming the divergence is symmetric, which would yield ~0.193 nats and incorrectly bypass the alert threshold.
realistic_solution: Compute the asymmetric D_KL(P||Q) by summing P(x) * ln(P(x)/Q(x)) over all bins, yielding 0.224 nats, which correctly triggers the drift alert.

title: Diagnosing Silent Accuracy Drops using KL Divergence
scenario: Your cloud-based recommendation model serves 10,000 requests/sec from a cluster of 8 T4 GPUs. Recently, the CTR dropped by 15% silently, with no system alerts firing. You compute the KL Divergence between the training embedding distribution (Q) and the production embedding distribution (P) for user features over a 1-hour window. The KL divergence spiked from its historical average of 0.05 nats to 1.2 nats. Analyze what this asymmetric metric implies about the incoming data pipeline, and diagnose the root cause of the CTR drop.
common_mistake: Assuming KL divergence is symmetric or that it directly correlates with overall feature importance, missing that KL(P||Q) heavily penalizes regions where the reference training distribution Q has near-zero probability but production P has mass.
realistic_solution: Analyze the direction of the KL divergence. A massive spike in KL(Production || Training) typically indicates the emergence of out-of-vocabulary (OOV) categorical features or a new user segment in production that had zero or near-zero probability in the training set. The model outputs random noise for these unknown features, tanking the CTR. You should switch to a bounded symmetric metric like Jensen-Shannon divergence for alerting and investigate the upstream data pipeline for missing validation constraints.
napkin_math: KL(P||Q) = Σ P(x) log(P(x)/Q(x)). If a new categorical feature appears in 2% of production traffic (P(x)=0.02) but was smoothed to a tiny probability in training (Q(x)=1e-6), the penalty for this single bin is 0.02 * log(0.02 / 1e-6) ≈ 0.02 * 9.9 ≈ 0.198 nats. If 5 such features emerge due to an upstream bug, the sum easily pushes the total KL divergence beyond 1.0 nats, triggering a massive shift signal.

title: Memory Overhead of Teacher Logits in LLM Distillation
scenario: You are setting up a knowledge distillation pipeline to train a 7B parameter student LLM from a frozen 70B parameter teacher LLM. Both models share a vocabulary size of 100,000 tokens. The training configuration uses a micro-batch size of 16 and a sequence length of 2048. Calculate the GPU memory required purely to materialize the teacher's output logits for the KL-divergence loss calculation, assuming FP32 precision is required for numerical stability during temperature scaling.
common_mistake: Candidates often calculate memory based solely on model weights, forgetting that the teacher's full vocabulary logit tensor must be materialized for every token in the batch, or they incorrectly assume FP16 is sufficient for the temperature-scaled Softmax.
realistic_solution: Calculate the dense logit tensor shape: [Batch, SeqLen, VocabSize]. Multiply by 4 bytes for FP32. This isolated memory requirement must be added to the teacher's weight memory (140GB in FP16) and the student's training state when provisioning the GPU nodes.
napkin_math:
Elements = 16 (batch) * 2048 (seq) * 100,000 (vocab) = 3.27 billion.
Memory = 3.2768e9 * 4 bytes = 13,107,200,000 bytes ≈ 13.1 GB.
13.1 GB is required strictly for the teacher logits per micro-batch.

title: Evaluating KL Divergence for High-Throughput Drift Detection
scenario: Your team serves a DLRM model at 10,000 QPS on a cluster of AWS Inferentia2 instances. To detect data drift, an engineer proposes computing the KL Divergence between the daily serving feature distributions (P, 864M samples) and the training distribution (Q). The features include heavily skewed continuous variables like session duration. Evaluate the robustness of using pure KL Divergence for this automated retraining trigger, considering edge cases like unseen feature bins in production.
common_mistake: Failing to recognize that KL Divergence is asymmetric and diverges to infinity if the reference distribution Q has zero probability for a bin where P > 0, causing false positive drift alerts.
realistic_solution: Reject pure KL Divergence in favor of the symmetric Jensen-Shannon Divergence (JSD) or Population Stability Index (PSI), or apply Laplace smoothing to the reference distribution Q to guarantee bounded outputs.
napkin_math: If bin i has P(i) = 0.01 and Q(i) = 0, D_KL = P(i) * log(P(i)/Q(i)) -> infinity. With Laplace smoothing epsilon = 1e-6, the contribution bounds to 0.01 * log(0.01/1e-6) = 0.01 * 4 * ln(10) ≈ 0.092 nats, avoiding system crashes.

title: Debugging Online KD Bottlenecks
scenario: Your team is performing online knowledge distillation from a 70B parameter teacher LLM to a 7B student LLM on an 8xH100 (80GB) node. The teacher is partitioned across 4 GPUs, while the student is trained on the remaining 4 GPUs using Data Parallelism. You observe that the student's GPU utilization is under 20%, and the overall training throughput is drastically slower than training the 7B model from scratch. What is the root cause of this inefficiency, and how should the system be redesigned?
common_mistake: Attempting to optimize the student's batch size or applying activation checkpointing, assuming the student's training loop is the bottleneck rather than the teacher's inference.
realistic_solution: The root cause is the synchronous online forward pass of the 70B teacher starving the 7B student. Redesign the system to use offline distillation by pre-computing the teacher's top-K logits (e.g., K=10) and storing them, decoupling the slow teacher inference from the fast student training loop.
napkin_math: Teacher forward compute is ~140 GFLOPs/token (2 * 70B). Student forward+backward is ~42 GFLOPs/token (6 * 7B). Synchronous execution forces the fast student to idle waiting for the teacher. Storing Top-10 FP32 logits takes only 40 bytes/token (10 * 4B). Streaming this from NVMe easily outpaces the teacher's compute-bound generation.

title: Evaluating Distillation Trade-offs for Cloud LLM Deployment
scenario: You need to deploy a real-time summarization model in the cloud. You currently have a highly accurate 70B parameter teacher model that requires at least 2xA100 (80GB) GPUs to serve due to memory constraints (approx 140GB in FP16). To reduce serving costs and fit the model on a single, cheaper GPU, you propose distilling it into a 7B student model. Evaluate the architectural trade-offs between standard logit-based distillation and intermediate-state distillation, specifically considering the 'capacity gap' and the impact of the temperature hyperparameter on the resulting student's ability to ca
common_mistake: Assuming higher temperature always transfers more knowledge, or ignoring the capacity mismatch where a 7B student lacks the representational power to mimic the 70B teacher's complex logit distributions.
realistic_solution: Evaluate the 10x capacity gap. Standard logit distillation might force the 7B student to mimic noise. Recommend combining task-specific hard labels with teacher soft labels at a moderate temperature (e.g., T=2 to 4), or employing intermediate feature matching to guide the student's hidden states, accepting the higher offline training cost for better online serving efficiency.
napkin_math: Serving 70B in FP16 requires ~140GB memory (2x 80GB A100). A 7B model in FP16 needs ~14GB memory, easily fitting on a single A100 or L4. Distillation overhead: running 70B forward pass over 10B tokens takes 70B * 2 * 10B = 1.4e15 FLOPs. At 120 TFLOP/s (A100), this takes ~3.2 hours of GPU time, an acceptable offline cost for >4x online cost reduction.

title: CUDA Graphs vs Fusion for Inference
scenario: You are evaluating optimization strategies for an LLM inference engine on NVIDIA A100 GPUs. The engine processes a custom attention block consisting of 80 sequential element-wise and reduction operations per layer. During token generation (batch size 1), profiling shows the GPU is idle 85% of the time despite 100% single-thread CPU utilization. The team is debating between implementing CUDA Graphs or writing a custom fused Triton kernel. The application requires continuous dynamic batching with frequently changing sequence lengths up to 4096. Evaluate the trade-offs between these two approache
common_mistake: Recommending CUDA Graphs because they are easier to implement, without considering that highly dynamic sequence lengths and batch sizes will force constant graph recompilation and massive memory overhead.
realistic_solution: Implement a custom fused Triton kernel. While CUDA Graphs effectively eliminate CPU launch overhead, capturing a separate graph for every unique batch size and sequence length combination is impractical for dynamic LLM inference. A fused kernel permanently reduces the launch count regardless of input shape dimensions.
napkin_math: 80 ops/layer * 32 layers = 2,560 kernel launches per token. At ~5µs CPU launch overhead per kernel, total CPU dispatch latency is 2,560 * 5µs = 12.8ms per token. Fusing the 80 ops into 1 kernel per layer reduces this to 32 * 5µs = 0.16ms, saving 12.6ms per token.

title: Evaluating KV Cache Memory Constraints and PagedAttention
scenario: You are deploying a custom 30B parameter LLM (FP16, 64 layers, 64 heads, 128 head dimension) on a node with 4x 80GB A100 GPUs (320GB total memory). The model weights consume 60GB across the GPUs. The product manager wants to guarantee a concurrent batch size of 128 users, each generating up to 2048 tokens. The serving team suggests using a naive static memory allocation for the KV cache to guarantee zero out-of-memory (OOM) errors during generation. Evaluate the feasibility of this proposal and justify a better architectural choice.
common_mistake: Failing to calculate the exact memory footprint of the KV cache, assuming OOMs are mostly caused by model weights, and overlooking the severe memory fragmentation caused by static contiguous allocation.
realistic_solution: Demonstrate that the maximum KV cache requires 512GB, heavily exceeding the 260GB available memory. Reject static allocation and propose PagedAttention (like vLLM) to dynamically manage memory in blocks, eliminating internal fragmentation and allowing for safe oversubscription or dynamic batching.
napkin_math: KV per token = 2 (K,V) * 2 bytes * 64 layers * 64 heads * 128 dim = 2MB. Max KV per user = 2MB * 2048 tokens = 4GB. Total KV for batch 128 = 128 * 4GB = 512GB. Available VRAM = 320GB - 60GB = 260GB. 512GB > 260GB.

title: Calculate Maximum Batch Size for LLM KV Cache
scenario: You are deploying a 13B parameter LLM (FP16 precision) on a single 80 GiB A100 GPU. The model has 40 layers, 40 attention heads, and a head dimension of 128. You need to support a maximum sequence length of 2048 tokens. After reserving 26 GiB for model weights and 4 GiB for forward-pass activations and workspace overheads, what is the maximum concurrent batch size you can serve before encountering out-of-memory (OOM) errors due to the KV cache bottleneck?
common_mistake: Ignoring the layer count or forgetting that both Keys and Values must be cached for every token, leading to a significant overestimation of the maximum batch size.
realistic_solution: Calculate the exact memory footprint of the KV cache per token per layer, multiply by the total number of layers and sequence length, and divide the available GPU memory by this per-sequence footprint.
napkin_math:
KV per token = 2 (K,V) * 40 layers * 40 heads * 128 dim * 2 bytes = 1,638,400 bytes (1.5625 MiB).
KV per sequence = 1.5625 MiB * 2048 tokens = 3200 MiB (3.125 GiB).
Available Memory = 80 GiB - 26 GiB - 4 GiB = 50 GiB (51200 MiB).
Max batch size = floor(51200 / 3200) = 16 sequences.

title: Diagnosing LLM Serving OOMs
scenario: You are serving Llama-2-70B (FP16 weights taking ~140GB) on a single 8x A100 40GB node (320GB total VRAM). To avoid reallocation latency, the serving engine statically pre-allocates the KV cache for the maximum sequence length of 4096 tokens for a target concurrent batch size of 128. During load testing, the system instantly throws CUDA OutOfMemory errors when attempting to serve 128 concurrent requests.
